rationale: Leading-digit concentration depends on how many distinct values are repeated and their magnitudes, which the repetition mechanism leaves free.
Terminal-digit deviation depends on the cents structure of the specific repeated values, not on repetition itself.
Cents entropy collapses only if the repeated values share cents endings; otherwise it tracks the underlying value set.
Modular-residue concentration arises only if the repeated values share a residue class, which is unconstrained by the mechanism.
Whether repeated payments cluster in time is set by contract scheduling, exogenous to value repetition.
The prime-identity spectrum reflects the factorization of the specific repeated integers, undetermined by repetition alone.
Ranked factorization shape depends on which integers are repeated, not on the fact of repetition.
Factorization tail mass is fixed by the chosen repeated values, not by repetition.
The convergence exponent depends on whether repeats are contiguous or interleaved, a sequencing parameter requiring simulation.
Compression-fit consistency depends on the ordering of repeats within the stream, which the mechanism does not fix.
Whole-unit rounding perturbs leading digits only weakly and in a magnitude-dependent way (Benford response flagged uncertain in the matrix remark).
Whether rounding reshapes the ranked factorization profile depends on magnitude regime and recording unit (L-profile effect uncertain).
Rounding injects factors of 2 and 5 whose tail-mass effect is magnitude-dependent and needs a finite-size baseline.
Compression asymptote depends on the residual diversity of rounded values, a tunable parameter.
The convergence exponent for rounded streams is not analytically predictable and requires simulation.
Focal-value concentration alters leading digits only through the specific focal set, a magnitude-dependent interaction.
Quarter/focal rounding's effect on factorization shape is magnitude- and unit-dependent, as with whole-dollar rounding.
Focal rounding (multiples of 25 cents) injects factors of 5 whose tail-mass effect is magnitude-dependent.
Compression diversity depends on how tightly values concentrate at focal points, a design parameter.
The convergence exponent requires sequential simulation of focal-concentrated streams.
Psychological pricing manipulates terminal digits/cents; leading digits are structurally insensitive except via incidental price-point clustering.
Whether .99-type endings perturb the prime-identity spectrum depends on the resulting integer factorizations at the recording unit.
The factorization-shape effect of psychological endings is magnitude- and unit-dependent.
Tail mass is not determined by the pricing-ending convention alone.
Compression diversity depends on the spread of psychological price points.
The convergence exponent requires simulation of psychologically-priced streams.
CBAD R2 degrades only if fiscal-period concentration coincides with a temporal regime change; for IID within-period amounts it stays stable, making this ordering-dependent.
Benford response is uncertain because digit effects depend on the interaction of the divisibility constraint with the magnitude distribution.
Terminal-digit effect depends on the divisor relative to base 10 (divisor 7 leaves terminal digits near-uniform; divisor 5 concentrates them).
Cents-entropy effect depends on whether the divisor shares factors with 100, requiring divisor-specific enumeration.
Leading-digit behavior of products depends on the factor distributions; products approach Benford but finite-size deviation is magnitude-dependent.
The prime-identity spectrum of products depends on the prime content of the factors, which composition leaves free.
Compression diversity of product-composed values depends on factor-set richness.
The convergence exponent for product streams requires sequential simulation.
A common fixed factor shifts leading digits multiplicatively; the resulting Benford deviation is factor- and magnitude-dependent.
The compression asymptote under concatenation depends on whether concatenated regimes differ in diversity (Delta a), a design parameter.
The convergence exponent under temporal concatenation depends on regime-boundary placement and ordering.
Any weak leading-digit perturbation from rounding is attenuated toward the aggregate's natural Benford behavior under summation (Prop 6 attenuation).
Whole-unit rounding is aggregation-closed (Prop 6): every sum of whole-unit amounts is whole-unit, so the terminal-digit signature is preserved.
Cents entropy stays minimal because sums of whole-unit values remain whole-unit (aggregation-closed, Prop 6).
Low-order modular residue (mod 100) is preserved under summation of whole-unit values (aggregation-closed, Prop 6).
Aggregation may wash out repetition or amplify it via recurring batch totals (Prop 6 amplification), depending on aggregation grouping.
Local threshold geometry attenuates under summation, but aligned batch caps could create new bunching (Prop 6).
Temporal concentration of aggregates depends on the reporting/batching calendar, exogenous to the rounding mechanism.
Preserved divisibility-by-base structure (and thus c_p elevation) holds only at a cents recording unit, making the spectrum signature unit-dependent (Props 5-6).
Ranked factorization shape of sums attenuates as components grow, but the finite-m residual shape is unknown (Prop 6 attenuation).
Factorization tail mass of sums attenuates under summation but the magnitude-dependent residual needs measurement.
Compression diversity of the aggregated stream depends on total diversity and batching structure.
The convergence exponent of the aggregated sequence requires simulation.
Fit consistency depends on whether aggregation introduces regime structure into the sequence.
The Benford response of divisible sums is magnitude- and divisor-dependent, as for the base divisibility mechanism.
Terminal-digit effect of summed divisible values depends on the divisor relative to base 10.
Cents-entropy effect depends on whether the divisor shares factors with 100.
Divisibility by C is aggregation-closed (Prop 6): sums of multiples of C remain multiples of C, so residue concentration is preserved (assuming C shares factors with the residue modulus).
Repetition concentration depends on whether aggregation produces recurring batch totals.
Local threshold geometry attenuates under summation but batch caps could re-bunch.
Temporal concentration depends on the batching calendar, exogenous to the divisibility constraint.
Divisibility by C is preserved under summation, but the magnitude-normalized c_p shrinks as sums grow, so the net spectrum signature is uncertain (Props 5-6).
The preserved divisor adds a fixed factor, but the ranked-shape effect at larger aggregate magnitudes is uncertain.
Preserved divisibility affects tail mass, but aggregate magnitude growth complicates the prediction.
Compression diversity of the aggregated divisible stream depends on diversity and batching.
The convergence exponent of the aggregated sequence requires simulation.
Fit consistency depends on whether aggregation introduces sequence regime structure.
